title: The Core of Calvinism, Part 1: The Sovereignty of God
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 7–7-24 - The Core of Calvinism 1 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Introduction: What Is Calvinism?
subpoints: Common misconceptions: predestination, no free will, frozen chosen
A 2007 newspaper article called Calvinism 'gloomy tenets'
The true Calvinist is one who lives for God's glory with great joy
Overview of the eight-week series

outline.title: The Marrow of Calvinism: The Sovereignty of God
subpoints: Not predestination, not the covenant of grace — but God's sovereignty is at the core
Sovereignty means rule; God is the Lord over all things
The story of Nebuchadnezzar illustrates the folly of self-sovereignty
Reformed faith is thoroughly theocentric — God is at the center of everything

outline.title: Calvinism Through Church History
subpoints: This is not a new or fringe movement; it has been the mainstream of Protestant Christianity
Augustine: 'You drove them from me, You who are the true sovereign joy'
Calvin himself demonstrated God-centeredness in his life and ministry
Jonathan Edwards: 'The enjoyment of God is the only happiness with which our souls can be satisfied'
Anne Griffiths, a humble Welsh farmer's wife, expressed the same God-centered passion on her deathbed

outline.title: The Practical Implications of God-Centeredness
subpoints: Sin is horrible primarily because it is an affront to God
True repentance begins with acknowledging that sin is first and foremost against God (Psalm 51:4)
Salvation is wonderful because it brings glory to God
Heaven is wonderful because God is all in all; hell is terrible because God's wrath is there
God is the most-used word in Romans — the greatest theological treatise

outline.title: The Loss of God-Centeredness in Modern Evangelicalism
subpoints: The self-esteem philosophy has produced a man-centered church
The fear of God has been lost; God is presented as 'user-friendly'
Materialism and worldliness flourish where there is little sense of a holy God

outline.title: Conclusion: Are We In Love With God Himself?
subpoints: The Calvinist is in love with the triune God, not just with what he can get from God
The ordinary means of grace — Scripture, prayer, corporate worship, fellowship — produce this enamoring
Colossians 3:16: being filled with the Word and speaking it to others deepens love for God
Preview of next session: the place of grace

summary: This first lesson in the eight-part "Core of Calvinism" Sunday School series asks a foundational question: what is the marrow — the very center — of the Reformed faith? Pastor Tito Lyro surveys common misconceptions about Calvinism (predestination, no free will, "frozen chosen"), then argues that neither predestination nor the covenant of grace is the ultimate center, but rather the sovereignty of God over all things. Using Romans 11:36 as a keynote text, the lesson defines Calvinism as a thoroughly theocentric system: God is the first cause, sustainer, and final end of all things, and the Calvinist is one who lives for God's glory with great joy. The pastor traces this God-centeredness through Augustine, Calvin, Jonathan Edwards, and B.B. Warfield, showing that it is the historic mainstream of Protestant Christianity. Practically, the lesson explores why sin is primarily an affront to God, why true repentance requires a God-centered framework, and why modern evangelicalism has drifted into man-centeredness. The session closes with a call to genuine love for God himself — cultivated through the ordinary means of grace — and previews the next session on sovereign grace.
transcript:
## Introduction: What Is Calvinism?

You see this slide on the screen? The Merrill Calvinism is not a Lord Merrill — it's a great word, a very tasty word. I grew up eating bowl marrow as far as daily menu at my parents' house, but that's not the kind of marrow that I want to talk about. When you talk about the marrow, the marrow is at the center of the bone, at the core of the bone. So I want us to talk about the core of Calvinism, and we're going to try that in an eight-week series, talking not so much about defining the doctrine of Calvinism, but how those doctrines that we're so familiar with work out in our lives as we live them out.

What answer would you get if you ask people on the street, what is Calvinism all about? Predestination. No free will. Frozen chosen. On Thanksgiving, 2007, a very well-known newspaper published an article regarding Calvinism with the title, "Thinking Like a Pilgrim on Thanksgiving." The article said this, talking about the pilgrims who are Calvinists: "their religious roots reached back to the gloomy tenets of John Calvin" — their over-simplification being that they lived with the nagging fear that they dangled every moment by a thin thread over the fiery pit of hell, in spite of their own faith or good works or the outward manifestations of the blessings of God.

I think a lot of us behave — at least we may not admit this objectively, but emotionally and psychologically — that somehow our salvation is always at risk, that regardless of what we believe, God might lose us at any moment, which is the exact opposite of what Calvinism teaches. If this is how you define Calvinism, I hope that for the next eight weeks we can change your mind, as we understand that the Calvinist or the Reformed believer is one who lives for God's glory with great joy. That's really what Calvinism is.

Next week we're going to talk about the place of grace. We'll talk about piety, about living for God's glory with great joy as it refers to worship, to evangelism, all of life, to family, and then to our freedom in Christ. That's the outline for the next seven weeks after today.

## The Marrow of Calvinism: The Sovereignty of God

So we wanted to start by asking ourselves, what is at the heart, what is the marrow of Calvinism? Some have suggested that the doctrine of predestination is at the heart of the reformed faith. This doctrine teaches that God in eternity past elected people to himself, and in history is bringing those that he chose in eternity past to himself. And yet that is not the heart of Reformed faith. Some have suggested the doctrine of the covenant of grace, which we love dearly. While the covenant of the relationship between God and man is emphasized in our theology, it is not necessarily the controlling concept.

The core, the marrow of Calvinism, is the sovereignty of God. This is really what the Bible is all about. That is really what our faith is all about. Sovereignty means rule. So to speak of God's sovereignty is to refer to God's rule. His sovereignty declares him to be God, the incomprehensible deity who is nevertheless knowable in so far as he chooses to reveal himself. He is the sovereignly gracious and omnipotent Yahweh of the Scriptures, the Most High who does his will in heaven and earth.

You know the story of Nebuchadnezzar? He saw himself as the sovereign one. He thought, look what I have done. I am the king of the world. I have done all these things. And then remember what happened? Seven years deranged, living in the woods. And then God restores him. And one of the first things that Nebuchadnezzar says after he's restored is this: "All the inhabitants of the earth are reputed as nothing. He does according to his will in the army of heaven and among the inhabitants of the earth. No one can restrain His hand or say to him, What have you done?" That is at the core of our theology because it is at the core of the Bible.

So this is the marrow of the Reformed faith. The Calvinist believes that God is the Lord of life and that he's sovereign over the universe. The Calvinist believes that God is free and independent of any force outside of himself to accomplish his purposes. God is not bound to anything or anyone other than himself. The Calvinist believes that God knows the end from the beginning, that he creates, sustains, governs and directs all things, and that his wonderful design will be fully and perfectly manifested at the end of the ages.

That's why Paul says in Romans 11:36, "For of Him and through Him and to Him are all things, to whom be glory forever and ever." All things come from God. All things are sustained through God. And all things exist to the end of going back to God. At the center of all this is God himself. So you can say that Calvinism or the Reformed faith is theocentric. The heart of the Reformed faith is the sovereign God.

A 19th-century pastor who was also an optometrist — Mason Pressley by name — said: "Just as a Methodist places in the foreground the idea of the salvation of sinners, the Baptist the mystery of regeneration, the Lutheran justification by faith, the Moravian the wounds of Christ, so the Calvinist is always placing in the foreground the thought of God." Brothers and sisters, God is more important than the salvation of sinners. God is more important than the salvation of our children. The most important thing in the Bible is not that people be saved. The most important thing in the Bible is that God be glorified.

## Calvinism Through Church History

You might think all of this is something new, just this church or a modern movement. But God-centeredness has been the trademark of the church throughout the centuries. Augustine confessed: "You drove them from me, You who are the true sovereign joy. You who are sweeter than all pleasure, who outshines all light, who surpasses all honor — all Lord, my God, my light, my wealth, and my salvation." His point was: if my will is so free to choose anything, why didn't it choose you earlier? You are so wonderful. Because his will wasn't free to choose God — it was bound to his sinful nature.

Calvin's successor Theodore Beza, having been a spectator of Calvin's conduct for 16 years, declared that "in him all men may see a most beautiful example of the Christian character." He spoke not of Calvin's theological accomplishments but of how God was his Lord and his life showed that.

Dr. Jonathan Edwards said: "The enjoyment of God is the only happiness with which our souls can be satisfied. To go to heaven, fully to enjoy God, is infinitely better than the most pleasant accommodations here. Fathers and mothers, husbands, wives, children, or the company of earthly friends, are but shadows; but God is the substance. These are but scattered beams, but God is the sun. These are but streams, but God is the ocean."

And lest you say this is only for great theologians, listen to the words of Anne Griffiths — a humble Welsh Calvinistic Methodist who was a farmer's wife who died in 1805 at age 29 giving birth to her first child. She said on her deathbed: "Gladly would I leave behind me all the idols I have known, since I bear and ascribe the likeness of a more exalted one. Where the unending worship, love, and reverence is he by his precious death were myriads from the jaws of death set free." This is a common farmer's wife — not a high-falutin theologian isolated from life.

B.B. Warfield, who cared for his invalid wife for decades, sincerely and honestly wrote: "The Calvinist is the man who sees God — God in nature, God in history, God in grace. Everywhere he sees God in his mighty stepping, everywhere he feels the work of his mighty arm. He casts himself on the grace of God alone, excluding every trace of dependence on self from the whole work of his salvation."

## Practical Implications of God-Centeredness

If God is at the center, then all doctrine must be defined in a God-centered way. Why is sin horrible? Not primarily because it hurts people, though it does. Not because it makes me feel bad, though it does. Sin is horrible because it is an affront to God. And if you don't realize that, then you are not able to repent truly, because true repentance starts with the acknowledgment that your sin is first and foremost against God.

David gives us an example of that when he says, "Against you, you only, have I sinned." Technically this is not literally true — David sinned against Bathsheba, against Uriah, against the nation, against his sons. And yet compared to the greatness of his sin against God, all these other elements seemed like nothing. The point is that ultimately it is against God and God only that we sin. If we don't have that fear of sin, that God is at the center, we are unable to truly repent.

Why is salvation wonderful? Not primarily because it frees us from hell, though it does. Salvation is wonderful because it brings glory to God. Heaven is wonderful because it is where God is all in all. Hell is terrible because it is where God pours out his righteous wrath. God is the most common word in the book of Romans — the greatest theological treatise of the New Testament. Not grace, not faith, not belief, not law, but God.

## The Loss of God-Centeredness in Modern Evangelicalism

This kind of God-centered passion has been mostly lost. I think because of our backsliding and the theological errors of our day. We now have the first adult generation raised under the self-esteem philosophy of education — a generation that has been told their whole life they're the best, they're number one, everybody owes them acknowledgment. That doesn't lend itself to the idea that no, you're not the best — God is, and unless you repent of your sins, he is your enemy.

In too many so-called evangelical churches, the fear of God has been lost, and with it a biblical understanding of the love of God. Evangelicalism has become man-centered and as a result promotes a view of God that is far less than the reality set forth in the Bible. Too many of us present God to others as more user-friendly than his word does. We want to make people feel comfortable, so we avoid telling them anything that will make them uneasy. We condone materialism and worldliness because we have so little sense of an ever-present, infinite, holy God.

## Conclusion: Are We In Love With God Himself?

As Calvinists, really at the bottom line, we are in love with God. Not just Jesus, but with God, the triune Yahweh of the Scriptures. We are overwhelmed by his majesty, his beauty, his holiness, and his grace. We seek his glory, we desire his presence, and model our lives after him. Other Christians say that evangelism or revival should be our greatest concern — and these things should greatly concern us for sure. But ultimately we have only one concern: to know God, to serve him, and to see him glorified. Everything else will fall into its place if that's our first goal in life.

So are we true sons and daughters of the Reformed faith who are enamored with God himself and with honoring and obeying him? Are we in love with God himself — not with what we can get through God?

The practical advice for growing in this love: faithfully studying the Scriptures, faithfully praying, above all faithfully worshiping God together with his saints, in the fellowship of believers. These are the things that will help us to be enamored with God more and more. It's incremental — like the ordinary means of grace. We may not see a difference day to day, but if we look two years ago, we see, wow, look what the Lord has been doing just through the weekly worship of God's Word, the sacraments, fellowship with his saints, praying, the preaching of the Word of God.

Colossians 3:16 is key here, where Paul says the Word of God will richly dwell in you — and he tells how: by actually speaking it to others. We grow in our love for Christ by actually speaking to others, spilling out the Word of God because we're so filled with it.

Let's pray. Father, thank you that you are a good God. We thank you that you reveal yourself to us in your Word and that you condescended — you came down to our level that we might know you. We thank you that the Lord Jesus Christ is the ultimate revelation of who you are and we thank you that your Spirit is working in us. Help us to love you. In asking Jesus name, amen.
